name: Simple Linear Regression
useCases: Predicting house prices based on square footage
Estimating sales revenue from advertising spend

description.description: Imagine drawing a straight line through a scattering of dots to show a trend. It predicts an outcome based on a single factor (like predicting ice cream sales based purely on the temperature outside).
The starting point of our line. For our ice cream example, it's how much ice cream you'd sell if the temperature was absolute zero.
description.example: If the intercept is 20, you would sell 20 units of ice cream even at 0°.

description.description: The slope of our line. It tells us how much the outcome changes when our factor goes up by one (e.g., selling 10 more ice creams for every 1 degree hotter).
description.example: A coefficient of 2.5 means each 1-degree rise in temperature adds 2.5 ice cream units in sales.

name: Multivariable Linear Regression
useCases: Predicting house prices using size, bedrooms, and location
Estimating employee salary from experience, education, and role
Forecasting energy consumption from weather, time, and building size

description.description: Like Simple Linear Regression, but using multiple factors at once. For example, predicting a house's price based on its size, number of bedrooms, AND age, rather than just size alone.
The baseline starting value before we consider any of our factors.
description.example: A base price of $50,000 for a house before factoring in bedrooms, sqft, etc.

description.description: The weights of importance assigned to each factor. It tells us exactly how much a specific factor (like adding an extra bedroom) impacts the final outcome.
description.example: If bedroom coefficient is $15,000, adding 1 bedroom adds $15k to the predicted price.

name: Logistic Regression
useCases: Email spam detection (spam vs. not spam)

description.description: Despite the name, this is used for sorting things into buckets rather than predicting numbers! It calculates the odds of a Yes/No outcome, like categorizing an email as 'Spam' or 'Not Spam'.
The baseline odds of an event happening before any evidence is gathered.
How much each piece of evidence tips the scales towards a Yes or a No.
Controls the trade-off between fitting the training data well and keeping the model simple. A smaller C makes the model more regularized (simpler).
description.example: C=0.1 gives a simpler model that may generalize better; C=100 fits training data tightly but may overfit.

description.description: The mathematical algorithm used to find the best coefficients. 'lbfgs' works well for small datasets, 'saga' is better for large ones.
description.example: 'liblinear' is great for binary classification with small data; 'saga' handles large-scale problems and L1 regularization.

name: KNN
useCases: Recommendation systems (movies similar to what you liked)

description.description: Like asking your closest neighbors for advice. If we want to classify a mystery fruit and its 5 most statistically similar neighbors are all apples, the algorithm guesses it's an apple too.
How many neighbors to ask! A small K might be swayed by a weird outlier neighbor, but a massive K might just blend everyone together.
description.example: K=3 is fast but noisy. K=15 is smoother but may blur class boundaries. Try odd numbers to avoid ties.

description.description: The math formula used to decide who your 'closest' neighbors actually are.
description.example: 'euclidean' = straight-line distance (good for numeric data). 'manhattan' = city-block distance (better when features have different scales).

description.description: An unsupervised sorting hat! If you dump a massive pile of unlabelled data on it, K-Means will naturally organize it into distinct groups by finding similarities, like sorting a messy room into categorized boxes.
The exact number of boxes you want to sort the data into. You have to tell the algorithm how many groups to look for.
description.example: K=3 for customer tiers (low/medium/high value). Use the Elbow Method to find optimal K.

description.description: How the algorithm chooses its initial starting points to begin grouping the data.
description.example: 'k-means++' picks smarter starting points (faster convergence), 'random' is simpler but less reliable.

description.description: A flowchart that makes decisions by asking a series of Yes/No questions. 'Is it raining? Yes -> take umbrella. No -> Is it sunny? Yes -> wear sunglasses.'
The mathematical rule the tree uses to decide what the best Yes/No question to ask is at any given step.
description.example: 'gini' (default) is faster. 'entropy' uses information theory and may give slightly different splits. 'log_loss' is good for probability estimation.

description.description: How many questions deep the flowchart is allowed to go. Stopping it early prevents it from overcomplicating things.
description.example: max_depth=3 gives a simple, interpretable tree. max_depth=20 captures complex patterns but risks overfitting.

name: Random Forest
useCases: Credit risk scoring and fraud detection
Disease outbreak prediction from health data

description.description: A democracy of Decision Trees. Instead of relying on one single flowchart (which might be biased), you build 100 slightly different flowcharts and let them all vote on the final answer.
The size of the voting committee. More trees usually means a better, more stable prediction, but it takes longer to calculate.
description.example: n_estimators=100 is a solid default. 500+ trees rarely improve accuracy but increase training time significantly.

description.description: How deep/complex each individual tree in the forest is allowed to grow.
description.example: max_depth=None (unlimited) lets trees fully grow. max_depth=10 is a good regularization starting point.

description.description: Imagine trying to draw the widest possible street between two different neighborhoods of data points. SVM tries to separate categories with as much "buffer room" as possible.
A mathematical trick that allows SVM to draw curvy or 3D boundaries if a straight line isn't enough to separate the groups.
description.example: 'linear' for text data (fast). 'rbf' for complex non-linear boundaries (most popular). 'poly' for polynomial decision boundaries.

description.description: A strictness dial. A high 'C' means the algorithm will bend over backwards to avoid misclassifying a single dot, while a low 'C' prefers a smoother, softer boundary.
description.example: C=0.1 allows some misclassification (better generalization). C=1000 forces near-perfect training accuracy (risk of overfitting).

description.description: A lightning-fast, probability-based guesser. It's "naive" because it assumes every piece of evidence is completely independent from one another (like assuming having the word 'Win' and having 'Cash' in an email are unrelated coincidences when aggressive spam filtering).
Telling the math whether it's looking at bell curves of continuous numbers, or distinct categories.
A small value added to prevent the model from assigning zero probability to unseen feature values.
description.example: var_smoothing=1e-9 (default) works well. Increase to 1e-5 if you have very sparse or noisy data.

name: DBSCAN
useCases: Detecting fraud clusters in financial transactions
Identifying noise and outliers in sensor data

description.description: An algorithm that identifies dense crowds of data points and ignores the isolated loners (noise). It's amazing for finding oddly shaped clusters, like predicting constellations in the sky.
The maximum distance two points can be apart for them to be considered 'in the same crowd'.
description.example: eps=0.5 finds tight clusters. eps=2.0 finds broader groupings. Use a k-distance plot to find the optimal value.

description.description: The minimum number of people required to officially constitute a 'crowd'.
description.example: min_samples=5 (default rule of thumb). For noisy data, increase to 10+. For sparse data, try 3.

name: ANN
useCases: Tabular data classification (customer churn, loan default)
Stock price prediction from financial indicators
Drug discovery and molecular property prediction

description.description: A virtual brain made of interconnected layers of 'neurons'. Starting with raw input, each layer passes signals forward and practices turning raw data into an intelligent guess.
The 'spark' inside a neuron that decides whether the neuron should fire or stay dormant based on the signals it just received.
description.example: 'relu' (most popular for hidden layers — fast, no vanishing gradient). 'sigmoid' for binary output. 'softmax' for multi-class output.

description.description: How deep the brain is. Adding more layers allows the brain to understand more complicated, abstract concepts.
description.example: 1-2 layers for simple tabular data. 3-5 layers for complex patterns. More layers = more training time and risk of overfitting.

description.parameter: Number of Neurons per Hidden Layer
description.description: The width of the brain. Adding more neurons in a layer allows the network to memorize more intricate patterns.
description.example: Start with 64-128 neurons. Tapering (128 → 64 → 32) often works well. Too many neurons can memorize noise.

name: CNN
useCases: Image classification (cats vs dogs, X-ray diagnosis)

description.description: A specialized brain designed specifically for looking at imagery. It scans images using small mathematical magnifying glasses to piece together shapes, textures, and eventually complex objects like faces or cars.
The magnifying glasses (filters) scanning the image to look for specific visual patterns like horizontal lines, corners, or colors.

parameters.description: How many different types of patterns the layer is searching for simultaneously.
parameters.example: 32 filters in early layers (detect simple edges). 128-256 in deeper layers (detect complex features).

parameters.description: The physical size of the magnifying glass sliding over the image.
parameters.example: 3x3 (most common, captures fine detail). 5x5 (captures broader patterns). 7x7 (used in first layer of some architectures).

parameters.description: How many pixels the magnifying glass shifts over with every step. Larger strides shrink the image faster.
parameters.example: Stride=1 preserves image size. Stride=2 halves the spatial dimensions (used instead of pooling in modern nets).

description.description: A simplification step. It deliberately compresses the image down by keeping only the most important recognized features, reducing the computational load.

parameters.description: The size of the window used to summarize and compress the image section.
parameters.example: 2x2 MaxPooling halves the image dimensions. AvgPool smooths out features.

description.description: Takes the 2D visual maps and squashes them into a single 1D line of data so a regular brain (Dense Layer) can analyze it.
A standard interconnected neural network layer that acts as the final decision-maker, taking all the visual clues and making the final guess.
Randomly turns off a percentage of neurons during training to prevent the brain from over-memorizing the exact training images.
description.example: Dropout=0.25 (light regularization). Dropout=0.5 (aggressive — recommended for small datasets).

name: XGBoost
useCases: Customer churn and lifetime value modeling

description.description: An extremely powerful, competitively tuned team of decision trees. It builds trees sequentially, where each new tree specifically tries to fix the mistakes made by all the previous ones. It's a favorite algorithm for winning Kaggle data science competitions!
How many sequential mistake-correcting trees to build.
description.example: 100 trees is a good starting point. 500+ for complex datasets. Monitor validation loss to avoid overfitting.

description.description: How aggressively each tree corrects the previous one's mistakes. Smaller values mean more conservative learning.
description.example: 0.1 (default, good balance). 0.01 with 1000+ trees gives best accuracy but is very slow. 0.3 for quick experiments.

description.description: Maximum depth of each individual tree. Controls model complexity.
description.example: max_depth=6 (default). 3-4 for simple data. 8-12 for complex interactions between features.

name: Gradient Boosting
useCases: Web search ranking (used by search engines)
Risk assessment in banking and insurance

description.description: Like a student learning from their mistakes. It builds a model, sees exactly where it failed to predict correctly, and builds another model focused purely on correcting those specific failures.
The total number of sequential error-correcting trees.
description.example: 100 (default). More trees improve accuracy but increase training time and overfitting risk.

description.description: Controls how much of a contribution each new tree makes. Smaller learning rates are slower but usually lead to better accuracy.
description.example: 0.1 (good default). Pair low learning rates (0.01) with high n_estimators (500+).

description.description: Complexity of each individual tree.
description.example: max_depth=3 (default, works well for most problems). Increase for complex feature interactions.

description.description: Teaching the computer to read text and determine the underlying emotional tone — whether the writer and text is happy, angry, or neutral. Great for automatically reading product reviews or monitoring social media pulses.
The maximum number of unique words the model considers from the vocabulary.
description.example: 5000 for small datasets. 20000-50000 for large diverse text corpora.

description.description: Controls model complexity, balancing training accuracy and generalization.
description.example: C=1.0 (default). Lower values create simpler models that generalize better.

name: Text Classification
useCases: Support ticket routing to correct department

description.description: A model designed to sort free-form text into predefined categories, like instantly organizing incoming news articles into 'Sports', 'Politics', or 'Technology' bins.
Size of the vocabulary considered by the model.
description.example: 5000 (quick and lean). 30000+ (captures nuanced vocabulary differences).

description.description: A smoothing parameter that prevents zero probabilities for unseen words.
description.example: alpha=1.0 (Laplace smoothing, default). alpha=0.1 for less smoothing when you have enough data.

name: ResNet (Residual Networks)
useCases: Fine-grained image classification (dog breeds, plant species)
Transfer learning for custom image datasets

description.description: A very deep foundational Convolutional Neural Network pretrained on millions of images. Think of it as an 'expert eye' that already knows how to detect edges, textures, and basic objects. Using skip-connections, it overcomes the vanishing gradient problem in deep networks.

When checked, the pretrained 'expert eye' remains locked, and only your newly added Dense Classification Head learns from the custom dataset. This is much faster and requires less data. Unchecking means 'Fine-Tuning', where the entire network adjusts its core vision to your specific images (slower, but potentially more accurate).
description.example: Frozen = great with <1000 images, trains in minutes. Unfrozen = needs 5000+ images, trains in hours but learns domain-specific features.

description.description: The custom decision-making layers attached to the end of the ResNet vision pipeline. This adapts the generic vision into your specific categories (e.g., Dog vs Cat, or X-Ray diagnosis).
The number of neurons in a hidden layer. More units allow learning more complex logic combinations, but increase the risk of over-memorizing the training dataset (overfitting).
description.example: 256 units for simple 2-class problems. 512-1024 for complex multi-class tasks.

description.description: The mathematical spark that allows the model to learn non-linear patterns. ReLU disables negative outputs, while Softmax converts final scores into percentages totaling 100%.

A regularization technique where random neurons are 'turned off' during training. This forces the network to learn robust, generalized patterns instead of relying heavily on a few specific neurons, drastically reducing overfitting.
description.example: 0.3 (light regularization). 0.5-0.7 (aggressive, use with small datasets or when validation loss diverges).

description.description: Accuracy (Percentage of correct predictions) and Categorical/Binary Crossentropy Loss (How confident the model was in its mistakes, lower is better).

name: LSTM (Sequence Forecasting)
useCases: Stock price and financial time-series prediction
Weather forecasting from historical sensor data
Natural language generation and text completion

description.description: A specialized Neural Network architecture designed for sequential data (like time-series, text, or audio). Unlike standard networks, LSTMs have internal 'memory cells' with gates (forget, input, output) that decide exactly what information to keep over time and what noise to discard.

The dimensional size of the internal memory state. A larger number of units means the LSTM can track more simultaneous patterns over time (like tracking temperature, humidity, and wind speed simultaneously).
description.example: 50 units for simple univariate time series. 128-256 for multi-feature sequences. 512+ for complex NLP tasks.

description.description: If TRUE, the LSTM outputs its memory state at every single time step in the sequence (required when stacking multiple LSTM layers). If FALSE, it only outputs the final summarized memory state after reading the entire sequence (usually set to FALSE for the very last LSTM layer before a Dense classification head).
description.example: Set to TRUE for all LSTM layers except the last one. Set to FALSE on the final LSTM layer before Dense output.

description.description: How many past time steps the model looks at to predict the next value.
description.example: 10 for daily stock data (10-day lookback). 60 for minute-level data. Higher values capture longer patterns but need more memory.

description.description: After the time sequences are analyzed, Dense layers interpret the final result. Activation functions (ReLU, Sigmoid) determine the output bounds of these interpretations.
Randomly drops connections between units to prevent the sequence memory from overly relying on highly specific chronological coincidences in the training data.
description.example: Dropout=0.2 (standard). Recurrent dropout=0.2 prevents the LSTM from relying on specific time-step patterns.

description.description: Commonly measures Mean Squared Error (MSE) or Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for forecasting numerical sequences, or Accuracy for classifying a sequence category.

name: YOLOv8 (Object Detection)
useCases: Real-time surveillance and security camera analysis

description.description: A state-of-the-art, single-shot real-time bounding box object detection model. It divides an image into a grid and simultaneously predicts the coordinates of boxes and the probability of classes inside those boxes in one swift pass.
The number of complete passes the YOLO network makes over the entire training dataset. YOLO often requires 50-100+ epochs to refine bounding box precision.
description.example: 50 epochs for fine-tuning a pretrained model. 100-300 for training from scratch.

description.description: How many images are passed through the network simultaneously before the weights are updated. Smaller batch sizes (8 or 16) are needed for high-resolution images to prevent Video RAM (VRAM) out-of-memory crashes.
description.example: batch=16 for 640px images on 8GB GPU. batch=8 for 1280px images. batch=32+ if you have 24GB+ VRAM.

description.description: The square pixel dimension that training images are resized to before processing (e.g., 640 for 640x640px). Higher resolutions allow detecting smaller objects but require exponentially more RAM and computing power.
description.example: imgsz=640 (default, good balance). imgsz=320 for fast experiments. imgsz=1280 for detecting tiny objects.

description.description: The initial step size the optimizer takes when adjusting bounding box weights. If set too high, bounding boxes will wildly bounce around the target. If too low, it takes forever to converge on the correct box.
description.example: lr0=0.01 (default for SGD). lr0=0.001 for Adam optimizer. Reduce by 10x when fine-tuning pretrained models.

description.description: The algorithm managing the weight updates. 'Adam' is usually safer for customized sets, while 'SGD' (Stochastic Gradient Descent) is standard for massive datasets.
description.example: 'auto' lets YOLOv8 choose. 'SGD' for large datasets (>10k images). 'AdamW' for small datasets with weight decay.

description.description: Mean Average Precision at 50% Intersection over Union. This measures what percentage of the predicted bounding boxes overlap with the real actual targets by at least 50%.
description.example: mAP50 > 0.7 is good. mAP50 > 0.85 is excellent. Compare mAP50-95 for stricter evaluation.

name: StyleGAN
useCases: Data augmentation for small image datasets
Fashion and interior design concept generation
Creating synthetic medical images for research
Deepfake detection research and adversarial training

description.description: An advanced Generative Adversarial Network that synthesizes hyper-realistic, high-resolution new images. It works by progressively generating imagery from low resolution up to high resolution, allowing unprecedented control over overarching layout ('coarse styles') down to fine textures ('fine styles').

Two neural networks fundamentally competing against one another: A Generator trying to forge fake images, and a Discriminator trying to act as an art-detective catching the fakes. Over time, the Generator becomes a master forger.
description.example: If Discriminator loss drops to 0 quickly → Generator is too weak (increase capacity). If Generator loss stays high → training is unstable (reduce learning rate).

description.description: The size of the initial random noise vector. Think of this as the size of the 'seed' of pure randomness that the network uses as a starting point to spark a specific generated image.
description.example: z_dim=256 (default). 512 for higher variety. Smaller values (128) generate less diverse but more stable outputs.

description.description: StyleGAN's biggest innovation. It takes the random 'Z' vector and maps it onto an unentangled 'W' space. This allows you to specifically alter one visual feature (like hair color) without accidentally changing another feature (like face shape).
description.example: w_dim=256 (match z_dim). Larger W dimensions allow finer style control but increase model complexity.

description.description: The mathematical target scale of the final generated image. A Log2 value of 6 generates 64x64px images, 8 generates 256x256px, and 10 produces 1024x1024px images. Higher resolutions require massive VRAM and take exponentially longer to train.
description.example: log_resolution=7 (128x128) for quick experiments. log_resolution=8 (256x256) for good quality. log_resolution=10 (1024x1024) for production.

description.description: Standard training loops parameters. For Generative modeling, you often deal with very small batch sizes (e.g., 4 or 8) due to high image VRAM footprints, compensated by very large epoch counts.
description.example: batch_size=4 with 1000+ epochs. Start with 64x64 resolution and fewer epochs to validate the pipeline works before scaling up.
